summary: Stancil argues that ChatGPT, like Google before it, rewards intuition and practice more than formal tricks, and that its real value today is speeding up tedious data work rather than generating novel analytical insight. Using Seth Stephens‑Davidowitz’s 30‑day AI‑assisted book as a case study, he says AI will flood us with faster, shakier analyses, making credibility and narrative framing even more decisive than the math itself.
money_quotes: People who are good at Googling aren’t good because they know that you search for keywords in URLs by using the “allinurl:” prefix; they’re good because they have an instinct for how Google works.
If we want to understand what our jobs might look like in several years, Who Makes the NBA? is the best answer we have yet.

In the end, most of the value that Stephens-Davidowitz got from ChatGPT was in accelerating the mundane and tedious tasks of cleaning and blending data, and in quickly cycling through dozens of regressions and pivots of those datasets.
Analysis isn’t an irrefutable science, but a kind of quantitative logical argument.
Just as Google made it possible to find an argument for anything, I suspect AI will eventually make it possible to create an argument for anything.
key_insight: AI speeds analysis but doesn’t solve judgment, so credibility and narrative cues will matter even more as conclusions become cheaper to produce.
